title: Command‑and‑Control (C2)
overview: A C2 server is attacker‑controlled infrastructure—often a public IP or domain name—hosting listeners that compromised machines (“agents” or “beacons”) call back to. It functions like a multi‑user Netcat listener, but with advanced payload management, tasking, and data collection. Common C2 frameworks include Cobalt Strike, Metasploit, Empire, Sliver, and Bru

study.overview: A C2 server is attacker‑controlled infrastructure—often a public IP or domain name—hosting listeners that compromised machines (“agents” or “beacons”) call back to. It functions like a multi‑user Netcat listener, but with advanced payload management, tasking, and data collection. Common C2 frameworks include Cobalt Strike, Metasploit, Empire, Sliver, and Bru
study.whySoc: Use this note as an analyst reference: understand the concept, identify the relevant telemetry, pivot through evidence, and document the decision path clearly.
workflow: Identify the user, host, process, network flow, or identity object involved.
Collect the highest-value logs first and preserve timestamps and assumptions.
Pivot through related notes, telemetry sources, and MITRE references.
Document findings, gaps, containment options, and detection improvements.

splunkExamples.code:
index=network*
| bin _time span=1m
| stats count by src_ip, _time
| streamstats window=5 stdev(count) as sd mean(count) as mu by src_ip
| where sd<5 AND mu>10

importantCommands.code: [Victim] → runs stager → [C2 Server] → delivers stage2 → [Victim loads full agent]

tips: - Avoid signature‑based detection: randomized patterns defeat static IOCs.
- Bypass behavioral analytics: mimic normal user/network activity.
- Delay discovery and response: longer dwell time means deeper compromise.
